chunk_summary: This section discusses the management of enteral failure due to oncology mucositis in high-risk pediatric patients, emphasizing timely parenteral nutrition as a rescue strategy and referencing key guidelines for infection prevention and nutrition support.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of parenteral nutrition in managing enteral failure caused by oncology mucositis in pediatric patients.
Identify the timing and indications for initiating parenteral nutrition in critically ill children.
Review guidelines for preventing catheter-related infections during parenteral nutrition.
Analyze the outcomes of early versus late parenteral nutrition in pediatric critical care.
key_facts: Oncology mucositis can lead to enteral failure, necessitating parenteral nutrition as a rescue intervention.
ASPEN/SCCM 2017 guidelines recommend timely initiation of parenteral nutrition in pediatric critically ill patients when enteral nutrition is not feasible.
The PEPaNIC trial highlights potential complications of early versus late parenteral nutrition in critically ill children.
The CDC provides guidelines to prevent intravascular catheter-related infections, a key risk during parenteral nutrition.
Parenteral nutrition should be carefully monitored to avoid complications such as bloodstream infections and metabolic disturbances.
reference_quote: "Guidelines for the provision and assessment of nutrition support therapy in the pediatric critically ill patient (ASPEN/SCCM 2017) emphasize the importance of timely parenteral nutrition in cases of enteral failure."
document_key_topics: Parenteral nutrition in pediatric critical care
